If pint relies on string parsing to find the prefix for scaled unit unit (does it), how can it tell if long or short scale[1] prefixes are used?

While even short scale countries seem to recognize [2] the proposed long scale units, does this cause grief on any other software? Is MMBtu any more of a unit than kilometer is? It may be more useful when talking about the scaled or derived units depending on the context, but these aren't base units themselves (I don't think you were trying to make this claim anyways).

I guess the rule for whether a derived unit like Mcf should be included in pint would be the same rule as whether mmHg or inHg count.

[1] https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Long_and_short_scales
[2] https://www.eia.gov/tools/faqs/faq.cfm?id=45&t=8

I'm no expert in data science, but I'm willing to be educated.

I think there's two ways to look at it.:

- A physicist/purist would make an argument that information is a fundamental physical quantity, and argue that it have its own unit.
- An engineer/pragmatist would try to take an inventory of all known applications, and determine what to do based on easiest implementation. 

The only situation where one implementation or another would make a serious difference would be bits in a numerator. For example:

```python
memory = 8 * u.bit
dynamic_range = 2 ** memory
```

Provided that the implementation of `__pow__` correctly handles this sort of operation (in a similar way to cosines with radians in #670), I think I'm slightly in favor of giving them an [information] dimension.

I had the same problem with the gzip parser, the `__byte_order__` parameter is the correct way to change endianess. No need to check for `sys.byteorder` however, because `'<'` and `'>'` are cross-platform for `struct.unpack` (see https://docs.python.org/2/library/struct.html#byte-order-size-and-alignment).

Note that if the endianess changes inside the same packet (unlikely), I guess that you need to set `__byte_order__ = ''` and then explicitly prepend `<` or `>` for each parameter (see https://github.com/kbandla/dpkt/blob/master/dpkt/dpkt.py#L37).

Keyring doesn't have a procedural resolution of keyring such that an if/else tree could be constructed to match that behavior. However, the priority order resolution with escape hatches should achieve what you describe:

- Give kwallet highest priority, raise RuntimeError if desktop is not KDE/Plasma.
- Give SecretService next highest priority.
- Give Gnome next highest priority.
- Give other backends lower priority.

And that is essentially what we have - kwallet.DBusBackend is 5.1, SecretService is 5.0, Gnome is 1.0, and other backends are generally lower, though some are not, such as pyfs which is 2.0.

Problem is, what about kwallet.QtKeyring? Should it be 5.0? Maybe it should also be downgraded to 1.0. What if secretstorage is available but not a suitable version?

Keyring attempts to solve this problem (or more accurately avoid explicitly solving the problem) by allowing each of the backends to independently declare their relative viability. If Gnome should generally be preferred over other backends that might be available (pyfs, keyczar), then we should raise its priority accordingly.

Okay, here is a first attempt at a report from the trenches...
It was a slow day at work, so I spent quite some time trying out the new version.

The target workstation(s) are Win7 with stock Python 3.3.5, and tightly locked down.
The only tools available are the good old REPL and Notepad, a dev's dream :-)
So I also had my own laptop on the side, which has Win10 and Python 2.7.11.

The good news is that when it works, it works quite well, especially with Py3.

The bad news is that, like previously, there was much hair-pulling and wondering what the heck was going on...
It seems to start working and then stop haphazardly from one run and the next, without any apparent difference
(although there are so many possible detail changes that it's often hard to tell).

Worse, it tends to blow up the python interpreter on a whim, on both platforms.
I got started ages ago with python 1.5, and in all those years I'm sure I've never seen so many REPLs give up the ghost on me in a single day :-)
I suspect that the ctypes interface to the underlying Windows DLL is quite brittle, and the slightest mismatch causes a crash.

There are some differences in the results between the two platforms, probably to the different versions of Windows, or Python, or both.

The Win10/Py27 setup crashed less, but unsurprisingly had more encoding issues.
At first the package wouldn't import because winkeyboard.py had accented characters in some comments, and no encoding declaration.
I use a language with accents, and as usual 90% of the time was spent trying to solve those problems, with mixed results.
Prefixing the hotkey parameter strings with u'...' did wonders for the majority (like à é è ç).
But try as I may, I have never succeeded with the circumflex accent (â ê û ô î), they just don't print.

> I definitely don't want to duplicate the one big computation (compute_true_positives) because it could potentially be a bottleneck as is. I personally like how I've currently implemented it, but I see how it's inconsistent with the existing mir_eval conventions. An exception that already exists is that the metrics called in melody.evaluate have a fair bit of preprocessing (melody.to_cent_voicing) happening beforehand as well, though it's closer to the raw input than the num_true_positives array...
Anyway, you decide what you want, and I'll update it accordingly :)

So, the distinction I would make is that all of the other metrics do non-evaluation preprocessing before being passed to metrics (if that makes sense).  There are a few places in `mir_eval` where the preprocessing wants both reference and estimated annotations, but it doesn't actually ever compare them in the sense of computing a metric.  For example, beat trims beat annotations completely independently of one other; segmentation does preprocessing independently except they pad estimated annotations to match reference; melody annotations are preprocessed independently except resample estimated annotations to match reference, etc.  So there is some kind of line there.  To me, the option which makes most sense is to combine all of these metrics into a single function - the actual core comparison being done is the same for all of the metrics, so I think it's OK that they are grouped.  There's also basically 0 overhead to computing all the metrics at once.  The disadvantage, of course, is that the function would return a ton of values, but I am OK with that.  I would sort of look at it like PRF metrics - we could potentially have separate functions for all three, but since they all are simple ways of reporting the same comparisons, they make sense to be grouped into a single function.  What do you think?

Nice find!

`if not hasattr(self, key):` was added for the case where a method returned a dict with a key of the same name as the method.  For example, `Movie.keywords()` returns a dict with a key called `keyword`.  If the attribute was set, then subsequent calls to `Movie.keywords()` would raise an error.

The problem with `if not hasattr(self, key):`, as you discovered, is that subsequent method calls don't reset the attributes, as they are already set.

To fix the problem for both issues, I added a check to see if the "attribute" is "callable()", i.e., 
`if not hasattr(self, key) or not callable(getattr(self, key)):`.

To confirm all is well, for now and in the future, I created a `tests/test_base.py` file.  I moved the multiple calls to `Movies.keywords()` test into that file and added another test for your `Search.movie()` example.

Yes, it does appear to correspond to that line, but the issue is whether an empty `scriptSig` stack can be valid. I believe the answer is "possibly". From <https://en.bitcoin.it/wiki/Script> (***emphasis*** added):

> A transaction is valid if nothing in the ***combined*** script triggers failure and the top stack item is true (non-zero).

It looks like the behavior of `eval_script` is correct (although I can't quite figure out why from looking at the code):

```py
>>> from pycoin.tx.script.vm import eval_script
>>> stack = []
>>> eval_script('', None, stack)
True
>>> stack
[]
```

So I think it is appropriate to close this, but I wanted to be clear as to why. On a side note, a ***combined*** empty stack looks like it will fail (as it should), specifically because `verify_script` will raise an `IndexError` (see [vm.py#L162](https://github.com/richardkiss/pycoin/blob/df0e3d9366332a261bc9998bff4ea38d25df08e5/pycoin/tx/script/vm.py#L162).

The destroy function doesn't initiate the end. It's a confusing name. There's also the stop and endLoop functions that are part of the engine stopping.

I did some searching and found that someone else working on SAPI5 with python had fixed a "lag" at the end of a speak. From what I could tell they used ISpAudio and setState to tell the SAPI5 driver to end the audio stream.

I added a line above line 57 in the sapi5.py file:
 Line 57: `self._tts.Speak('', 3)`
Line I added above Line 57: `self._tts.ISpAudio.SetState(1,0)`

So, the stop function in sapi5.py should look like this:
```
    def stop(self):
        if not self._speaking:
            return
        self._proxy.setBusy(True)
        self._stopping = True
        self._tts.ISpAudio.SetState(1,0)
        self._tts.Speak('', 3)
```

Could you see, if that solves your problem?

This is actually user error, but I only noticed that halfway through writing that this was definitely a bug in Hypothesis, so there's at the very least a bug that this is super confusing!

What's happening is that Hypothesis has given up after it's tried a certain number of examples, and is then raising an Unsatisfiable error saying that not enough of them met the criteria because the probability of the assume hitting is too low. adaptive assume will start to kick in and find you examples, but it needs a bit more to work with than it's got here.

You can try to fix this by raising max\_examples in Settings higher. The default is 200, and I tend to run with 1000 or 2000 for hard to find cases. You could also try replacing some of your uses of floats with floats_in_range, as that will not have the same problem of difficult to hit criteria.

我们想发消息给公众号，一般就是有公众号名字，再对这个名字发送字符。但是在代码里，要向公众号发送消息需要公众号的ID。在有了公众号列表之后，只需要在这里面通过名字查找ID，即可实现发送消息的目的。

```
#获取公众号ID
def getPubID(PubName):
    publicList = itchat.__client.storageClass.publicList
    for m in publicList:
        if (PubName == m['NickName']):
            return m['UserName']
    return False
```
再定义给公众号发消息的函数：
```
#发送消息到指定的公众号

    pid = getPubID(PubName)
    itchat.send(Word, pid)
```

Hi Patrick,

I see the rationale behind this feature but I don't think it is something that is often needed. For this reason I wouldn't like to sacrifice the type safety of the operators of the `Flags` class.

If I added this feature I would probably add it as a method to the flags class. If your project needs this feature often you can create your own `Flags` base class:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

from flags import Flags


class FlagsBase(Flags):
    def all_or_nothing(self, all):
        return self if all else self.__class__.__no_flags__


class TestFlags(FlagsBase):
    member0 = ()
    member1 = ()


def test(expr):
    print('%s: %s' % (expr, eval(expr)))


test('TestFlags.member0.all_or_nothing(True)')
test('TestFlags.member0.all_or_nothing(False)')

test('TestFlags.member1.all_or_nothing(True)')
test('TestFlags.member1.all_or_nothing(False)')

test('(TestFlags.member0|TestFlags.member1).all_or_nothing(True)')
test('(TestFlags.member0|TestFlags.member1).all_or_nothing(False)')
```

@nadlerjessie @spulec @hltbra As far as I know, yipit/awslimits is the only public non-trivial consumer of the awslimitchecker Python API. This PR adds support for the Route53 Hosted Zone limits, which differ from all other limits we handle to date because the actual limit/threshold is specific to each Hosted Zone. The current implementation here means that if an account had 30 hosted zones, we'd be adding potentially 60 limits: "foo.com Record Sets" for each zone and and "foo.com VPC Associations" for each Private zone.

Any thoughts from your perspective on:

1. Just add the limits.
2. Add them but with a specific subclass of AwsLimit, so they can be filtered/handled differently by the API consumer if desired.
3. Rework the internals of AwsLimit to handle limits like these (where the actual numeric limit can differ between each use of it), which would likely end up in some public API changes (or at least changes in output formatting)

Hi all,
In both windows and linux the json file is always stored in the directory from where you have run the CLI.

I know it is possible to retrieve the contents of a tweet if you know the username and id with "https://twitter.com/<username>/status/<tweet-id>".
And I was wondering if it is possible to retrieve the contents of a tweet by id only. I have looked on the internet and I could not find a good answer. 
If this is not possible, and the original tweet is not in the list of scraped tweets, the reply_to_id is not very useful. 

One way in which you can find out the username belonging to the original tweet is with the following command:
` reply_to_users = json.loads(tweet.find('div', 'tweet')['data-reply-to-users-json']) or [] `

This retrieves a JSON-list containing among other things the username, screen_name and id_str of everyone which has participated in the conversation. 
If the list has a length of 1, the tweet was not a reply to anyone and the list only contains information about the current tweet. 
If the list contains more than 1 element, the tweet was a reply, and the last element in the list contains information about the user of the original tweet. 

If it is not possible to retrieve a tweet by id only, I suggest you also include the username of the original tweet.

It's actually not a bug, that's the intended way the Socket.IO protocol works. The idea is that events can have a list of arguments. If you pass a list, then these are expanded as independent arguments. If you pass a list in which the first element is a list, then that inner list is given as the first and only argument to the function on the other side.

It's because the `OpenAPI Renderer` is using `CoreAPI Renderer` to supply `Swagger` with schema.
The problem is, `CoreAPI` does not support nested fields definition.

However, DRF already provides a full schema as metadata.
http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/metadata/

It seem to also be used internally for the Browseable API.
The way `OpenAPI` is generated should use metadata instead relying on the `CoreAPI Renderer`.

@kunalparmar I'm not exactly sure what you mean about making all datasets deferred? In the current genty, and in code that is currently using genty, code looks like:

```
@genty_dataset(... logic that builds the parameter sets ...)
def test_something(self, ... parameters...):
      ...
```

The "logic that builds the parameter sets" is evaluated at module load time. That can't change. And changes/enhancements to genty can't make that work differently. So all datasets can't be changes to be deferred.

In you sample code I don't see exactly where the datasets are defined? If what you mean is this:

```
@genty_dataprovider
def _provide_api_response(self):
    # this function return a sequence of results... each item of which is a "dataset", a la:
    yield dataset1
    yield dataset2
    ...
```
then that won't work. The datasets (or at least the data that goes into creating the final parameters given to the test method) must be defined at module import... because that's when the Test Class is constructed... so that's when genty needs to *create* the N test_something_<variants>, such that the subsequent test runner can "find" them and then start executing them.

If you meant something different, can you please clarify? Thanks!

Sorry, had to re-open. I realized that `request.META.get("REMOTE_ADDR", None)` return blank specially if your app is running in wsgi server  like gunicorn. We should try to get the value of HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR first and if there's none, then get REMOTE_ADDR.

Here's my fix.

views/comments.py
original
`    comment.ip_address = request.META.get("REMOTE_ADDR", None)`

modified
```
    x_forwarded_for = request.META.get('HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR')
    if x_forwarded_for:
        ip = x_forwarded_for.split(',')[0]
    else:
        ip = request.META.get('REMOTE_ADDR', None)

    comment.ip_address = ip

```

I just had a longer think about this, and am wondering if instead the correct 'fix' is the other way around: update the mocks to produce bytes instead of strings.

The argument here is that by passing bytes from Spark through to the output we avoid any and all encoding issues. The only exception here is in `yarn-cluster` mode, where Airflow wants to detect the application id of the job that was submitted. In _this_ case the previous code would use a best effort to get it out: assume (hope?) the data was UTF-8 encoded (which is pretty reasonable) but only for the purpose of extracting the application id. In the case it was wrong, the original data was still passed through as-is and the only harm was that Airflow would miss the application id; the log data would still be fine.

Earlier, you pointed out linkify is broken and I agreed and will look into it. So let's leave that out for now and just talk about `bleach.clean`. Bleach 2.0 and previous versions convert `&` to `&`: ``` > python Python 2.7.13 (default, Jan 19 2017, 14:48:08) [GCC 6.3.0 20170118] on linux2 Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information. >>> import bleach >>> bleach.clean('http://example.com/?this=that&foo=bar') u'http://example.com/?this=that&foo=bar' >>> bleach.clean('some link') u'some link' ``` That doesn't change with this PR. What does change is this case: ``` > python Python 2.7.13 (default, Jan 19 2017, 14:48:08) [GCC 6.3.0 20170118] on linux2 Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information. >>> bleach.clean('http://example.com/?this=that¤t=bar') u'http://example.com/?this=that\xa4t=bar' ``` In Bleach 2.0 and previous, `¤` is interpreted as a character entity and changed to its unicode value. The change breaks the url. With this change, those three examples become this: ``` Python 2.7.13 (default, Jan 19 2017, 14:48:08) [GCC 6.3.0 20170118] on linux2 Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information. >>> import bleach >>> bleach.clean('http://example.com/?this=that&foo=bar') u'http://example.com/?this=that&foo=bar' >>> bleach.clean('some link') u'some link' >>> bleach.clean('http://example.com/?this=that¤t=bar') u'http://example.com/?this=that&current=bar' ``` Those links look right and work fine in a browser. Maybe I don't understand what you mean by urls broken by this change. Can you show me some examples of urls broken by `bleach.clean` changes in this PR?

What I meant by listing the permutations was something a bit more detailed, like:

1) Posting to the jobs endpoint
  (a) Posting a job with a log URL set but no artifacts [this is what the buildbot jobs do]. So we need to generate everything.
  (b) Posting a job with a log URL set, with parsed status set to parsed, a text_log_summary artifact, but no "Bug suggestions" artifact [the desired future state for Taskcluster]. So we need to generate only the "Bug suggestions" artifact.
  (c) Posting a job with a log URL set, with parsed status set to parsed, a text_log_summary and a "Bug suggestions" artifact, so no further generation of artifacts required [this case is likely not to be used by many submitters]

2) Posting to the artifacts endpoint
  (a) Posting just a text_log_summary artfact. We need to generate the "Bug suggestions" artifact if it doesn't exist (in case it was somehow submitting prior to the text_log_summary artifact), but not if it doesn't. [Not sure how many submitters will hit this case, since many will just provide the artifact along with the job, on the jobs endpoint].
  (b) Posting both a text_log_summary and a "Bug suggestions" artifact. No further artifact generation required. [This is the case that our log parser will hit]

I've also noticed that we don't currently enforce that someone doesn't submit a job with a log parsed status of "pending" but also provide a text_log_summary - since at the moment, we'll re-parse the log anyway. Or alternatively, I wonder if we stop allowing them to specify the log parse status themselves, and we make the determination based on whether they provide the "text_log_summary"?

By listing the permutations out above, it seems that we're missing a few tests (this is what I meant by https://github.com/mozilla/treeherder/pull/518#issuecomment-101073108):
* Checking that posting a job to the jobs endpoint with both a text_log_summary and "Bug suggestions" artifact doesn't try to generate another "Bug suggestions" artifact.
* Checking that posting both a text_log_summary and "Bug suggestions" artifact to the artifacts endpoint doesn't try to generate another "Bug suggestions" artifact.
* Checking that posting a job to the jobs endpoint with a parsed status of "parsed" doesn't try to parse the log.
* Checking that posting a job to the jobs endpoint with a parsed status of "pending" but with an artifact called "text_log_summary" is either disallowed or we update the parse status to "parsed" and don't re-parse the log.

There are a couple of ways, sorted by effort (and payoff).

You can have Python run the package as a module:

```
$ python --version
Python 2.7.12
$
$ pwd
~/proj/cloudflare
$
$ python -m cli4
usage: cli4 [-V|--version] [-h|--help] [-v|--verbose] [-q|--quiet] [-j|--json] [-y|--yaml] [-r|--raw] [-d|--dump] [--get|--patch|--post|--put|--delete] [item=value ...] /command...
```

You can make an editable install, this will make the package act as in production but you can edit it live without having the reinstall to test your changes. The --user option installs the package in ~/.local/:

```
$ pwd
~/proj
$
$ pip install --user --editable cloudflare
Successfully installed cloudflare
$
$ python -c 'import CloudFlare; print CloudFlare.__file__'
~/proj/cloudflare/CloudFlare/__init__.py
$
$ cd ~
$ python -c 'import CloudFlare; print CloudFlare.__file__'
~/proj/cloudflare/CloudFlare/__init__.py
$
$ cli4
usage: cli4 [-V|--version] [-h|--help] [-v|--verbose] [-q|--quiet] [-j|--json] [-y|--yaml] [-r|--raw] [-d|--dump] [--get|--patch|--post|--put|--delete] [item=value ...] /command...
$ which cli4
~/.local/bin/cli4
```
Or my preferred method, virtualenv. Then you have a clean environment that is easy to scratch. If you like this way of working you can look up virtualenvwrapper later to make it more convenient. Because the virtualenv is empty of Python packages installed globally or locally this is the only method with which one would have found the setup.py dependency issue.

```
$ pwd
~
$ virtualenv ~/venvs/cloudflare
Installing setuptools, pip, wheel...done.
(cloudflare) $ pip install --editable proj/cloudflare
Successfully installed cloudflare future-0.16.0 logger-1.4 pyyaml-3.12 requests-2.12.4
(cloudflare) $ which cli4
~/venvs/cloudflare/bin/cli4
(cloudflare) $ pip freeze
-e git+git@github.com:yesbox/python-cloudflare.git@c0133052de575c2e277d51643b8c8d39bbf96fe3#egg=cloudflare
future==0.16.0
logger==1.4
PyYAML==3.12
requests==2.12.4
(cloudflare) $ deactivate
$
$ cli4
-bash: cli4: command not found
```

Correct -- I did not have to include this patch once I wrapped the incoming stream in an object that has a `seek()` method. I worked around the md5 checksum requirement by disabling it (in my use case, the md5 hash is already pre-computed for the upload and computing it twice does not make sense). After disabling the md5 check, the only time `seek` is called is on retry with `seek(0)`, which I can do by reopening the incoming stream (or boto could not retry if `seek()` is not implemented). Having explored this, I don't think there are that many layers that fail, but I understand the reluctance to fix this issue.

The `upload_fileobj` suggestion does not seem like something I'd want to use, however. The stream is non-seekable because it's a stream from another storage system. Using multipart uploads for it is problematic, because the ETag computation becomes different from the md5 hash already provided. Hence, for smaller objects I would want to disable the MPU behavior (by setting it to the maximum object size), but buffering the entire object in memory would not work very well.

First, I don't think this discussion should block getting this merged.  The fix as is, is good and we can address the `set_exception`  behavior in a separate thread.  But I still think the existing behavior is confusing, assuming I'm not missing anything.

From what I understand:

* A thread comes along and cancels the task.
* Whether or not that cancellation "sticks" depends on what happens in the final task.
* If the final task succeeds, then `set_result()` overrides and the cancel for that task is essentially ignored.
* If the final task fails (say API error or something), then the cancellation sticks and we ignore the error raised in the final task.

That still seems like a weird consistency to me.

the correct usage is `"in 30 days [at]? <time>"` or `"30 days ago [at]? <time>"`
I think negative 'days' values shouldn't be accepted since -30 days ago sounds invalid from natural language issue. 

But then what exactly happens - to `'-30 days at midnight'`?
(reference file: freshness_date_parser.py)  

Basically, it does identify -30 days unit as a relative time 
So it takes primary reference `now` and tries add -30 days. But since neither `in` nor `ago` is present the operation fails - and somehow due to this, the component 'midnight' is no longer processed. what is returned is neither 30 days (added/subtracted) NOR does it apply 'midnigiht'.

To be sure: `'in -30 days at midnight'` does returns a date 30 days in advance (not behind) but puts current time. 
If you apply `"in 30 days at midnight"` actually returns both currect date and time as `"00:00"` time so -ve sign is what is affecting "midnight" part. 

However, according to me, if `in` and `out` are both missing or having -ve sign it should simply refuse to output which will be rather correct behaviour. 

In either case, if the input is invalid or vague it is always an open ended debate whether software should do best it can, or should out right reject.

I see. I would agree if we were talking about twisted proper, but I do feel having a more 'reliable' behavior for treq.collect which even within treq is a higher level API and already employs the deliverBody mechanism. This behaviour could be disabled by default but easy to for a user to employ with an extra keyword argument (such as a per-chunk timeout), would be preferable. 

I will concede that requests with stream=True behaves exactly the same way, and the reason I noticed it with treq is because in this particular application I care about the total number of parallel connections with unreliable networks and was seeing a potential for my fairly small pool of connections to get locked up on dead downloads. At the same time, though, most browsers seem to be smart enough to figure out that a download is dead when the network goes on walkabout. 

Assuming I do get the deliverBody approach to work (if I do it'll be in the next day or two at most), I would like to post it as a comment here for reference in case anyone else comes around looking for such a solution. I would also be open to submitting the same or similar as a pull request to treq over the next few weeks, which allows this behavior to be turned on by a user, if there is interest in having it present within treq. Modulo that, this issue can be closed.

@uber1geek The only reason I see in the source code why you might receive an Invalid Token error message is when your payload does not contain a username field.

Django-rest-framework-jwt by default checks that a username field is included in the payload. You can see this in the method in class `BaseJSONWebTokenAuthentication.authenticate_credentials` and the method in class `VerificationBaseSerializer._check_user`. 
This is to ensure that the payload contains expected user information and identify a user.

You probably will need to create your own `VerifyJSONWebTokenSerializer` and `RefreshJSONWebTokenSerializer` and overwrite the method `validate`, and use these two serializers on your API to validate the token send in the http request. Because you don't have a username field in your payload, this method raises the ValidationError you can see as "Invalid payload".

> Sure. I'm biased, since most of the C projects I've participated in are explicitly C89-compatible.

Technically brotli already isn't C89-only.  It uses the types from `stdint.h` (i.e., `uint8_t`), except for MSVC < 16 where it defines those types itself, which is almost exactly (only the version of MSVC is different) what I'm suggesting it do for `stdbool.h`.

To make matters worse, the exact-size types (`(u)intN_t`) technically aren't even required by C99 (see § 7.18.1.1).  Only the minimum-width and fast minimum-width types (`(u)int_leastN_t` and `(u)int_fastN_t`) are required.  That said, everyone supports them; AFAIK they're really just option to support `CHAR_BIT != 8`, and I doubt Brotli would work if `CHAR_BIT != 8` anyways.

> I also define "portable" as "capable of having C++ bindings written for it", and bool has (or used to have?) issues there too. Hopefully we've gotten to the point where C++ and C toolchains targeting the same architecture agree on the size of bool, but IIRC, it used to be a problem... maybe someone with more recent experience can comment on this?

I'm not really a C++ person, but IIRC they have a bool type which is (can be or must be, not sure which) implemented with an enum.  I'm not sure whether it is still a problem, but to get around it you can use `_Bool` (which is built-in, doesn't require a header) instead of `bool`.  Maybe it would be safer to define `BROTLI_BOOL` as _Bool instead of bool, in which case you could just skip including `stdbool.h` altogether.

> > Being stuck with C89 in 2016 is ridiculous.
> 
> Unless you're serious about being portable to a C89 toolchain subset, yes. ;) I mean, I hate old toolchains just as much as the next guy, but this is the game you play when authoring a C ABI for the masses. (And having a C89-subset ABI doesn't necessarily mean that you have to write your implementation in C89.)

I don't care much about being portable to a generic, strictly C89 compiler.  I care about being portable to the toolchains people actually use, even relatively old ones.  C89 is mainly useful as a lowest-common-denominator, and if we want to use features outside C89 we should do so in a portable way.  The tiny areas of Brotli (like booleans) which aren't strictly C89-compliant can still be portable while providing a good API for people who have better compilers.

> 
>     virtually everyone other than VS < 2013
> 
> I'm veering off-topic, but I disagree with your assertion that the users of old MSVC versions are "a tiny group of people". MSVC9 and MSVC11 were pretty popular the last time I checked (which, to be fair, was a year ago... YMMV).

You're right, I didn't mean to include old MSVC in that "tiny group".  It's part of a separate group which can easily be special-cased (and, in every implementation I've suggested, is).  There are four groups

 * C99 compilers and compilers which support _Bool and stdbool.h, *even in C89 mode*.  This includes GCC >= 3, MSVC >= 18.0, and any compiler masquerading as one of them.
 * MSVC < 18 (a.k.a. VS 12 / 2013).
 * Everything else where `sizeof(_Bool) == 1`
 * Everything else.

The first group can be taken care of by simply using `stdbool.h` or `_Bool`.  Virtually every compiler pretends to be either GCC or MSVC (by defining things like `__GNUC__` and/or `_MSC_VER`).  The major exception is older versions of MSVC…

The second group is easy to take care of by defining either `BROTLI_BOOL`, `bool`, or `_Bool` to a type compatible with what later versions of MSVC use for `_Bool`.  I don't have a Windows box with VS to test this with, but mingw says `sizeof(_Bool) == 1`, so I think `unsigned char` should work.

What's left, the third and fourth groups, is what is extremely tiny.    You're really just left with toy compilers and probably a few special-purpose compilers targeting weird architectures.  TBH I'd be surprised if the rest of Brotli worked here without any effort, but with what I've been suggesting these platforms would still work as the boolean type would just be `#define`d as `unsigned char`.

Finally, you have the group of compilers not pretending to be GCC or MSVC on platforms where booleans use multiple bytes of storage.  If you compile Brotli with the same compiler as your application then the boolean type will be `unsigned char` and you're done.  If, however, Brotli is compiled with a different compiler all you would have to do is define the boolean type to something compatible before including Brotli.

That covers every platform I can think of, which pretty much checks the "portability" box as far as I'm concerned.

----

So, just in case the C++ issue @jchampio mentioned, how about this:

```c
#if !defined(BROTLI_BOOL)
#  if (defined(__STDC_VERSION__) && __STDC_VERSION__ >= 199901L)) || \
      (defined(__GNUC__) && (__GNUC__ >= 3)) || \
      (defined(_MSC_VER) && (_MSC_VER >= 1800))
#    define BROTLI_BOOL _Bool
#  else
#    define BROTLI_BOOL unsigned char
#  endif
#endif
#define BROTLI_TRUE 1
#define BROTLI_FALSE 0
```

Another option would be to use the build system to determine the value of `sizeof(_Bool)` and write an appropriate type, but that would require running an executable compiled for the target architecture, which would be a problem for cross-compilers.

I noticed that the hlr test is really slow.  It takes 11 seconds currently, which is a lot for a single test.  I have a number of optimization suggestions that can speed it up -- I managed to get it down to 1.7 seconds so far.

But it got me thinking about the part where you take nstep random walk steps.  You say you really want to be in the limit of nstep >> 1, and 40 is a compromise for speed.  But I'm pretty sure we can go all the way to nstep = infinity analytically.  Then the position of the next point relative to the current one is distributed as a Gaussian.  That's basically going to the limit of this being a diffusion problem.

So I think rather than doing nstep random walk steps of size `scale`, we can take a single step where dx and dy are each Gaussian distributed with some `sigma`. (I believe the equivalent sigma is `sigma = sqrt(nstep/2) * scale` using your current calculation.)

This would significantly speed it up, since it would save a bunch of trig calls (now needing none) and rng() calls (just two per point).  And presumably, the results would be better, since we'd be firmly in the limit of nstep >> 1, which is apparently preferable.

The behavior is apparently somehow triggered by the `window_position` property being set to `center-always` in prefs.glade (along with the window being resizable). Setitng it to `center` fixes it in both desktop environment where I've encountered the problem: the prefs window is centered initially, but later you can move/resize as you wish.

Apparently `center-always` doesn't work well with a resizable window. The window manager wants to resize it at one corner, leaving the opposite corner in place. The window wants to keep re-centering itself, resulting in the opposite corner moving too. These two contradict each other, and that fight somehow results in the crazy behavior.

autohint.py will call 'tx' in a number of workflows, so it can't safely run if 'tx' can't be found. You are correct that every script which uses 'tx' or another tool could reference by a path relative to the current script.  However, doing that leads to some extra work if the location of 'tx' or of the calling scripts ever changes. I would think it is simpler to just override the PATH variable in your batch file by adding the desired FDK path to the beginning of the PATH string. Then you don't need to reference the absolute path in all the other lines:
set PATH=C:\FDK\Tools\win;%PATH%
autohint -h

Also, in a most of these places, the code could be somewhat simplified by using the python [six](http://pythonhosted.org//six/) library. It includes functions for iteritems (`six.iteritems(dict)`) / itervalues (`six.itervalues(dict)`), xrange (`six.moves.range()`), and checking string types (`isinstance(obj, six.string_types)`)

I can go back and re-write the changes using that instead, but I wasn't sure if the autoprotocol maintainers would rather avoid the extra dependency.

In combination with the fact that pex synthesizes a custom `__main__.py` that forwards to the underlying entrypoint, ansible's strategy of importing the display from the `__main__` module as a hack to get a global display object fails. This import from `__main__` and fallback to constructing a new `Display` object is pervasive in ansible modules and it means verbosity settings fail to stick when the global import hack fails, which it ~uniquely does in pex.
This is now done https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pex/1.0.1

@scanny can you explain more?

I'm inclined to think the problem you're describing is caused by writing long stretches of code rather than modularizing your code into functions or objects and methods. If you have a function like this:

```python
def write_document(filename):
    document = Document()
    table = document.add_table(...)
    ... operate on document ...
    document.save(filename)

write_document('demo.docx')

table.cell(0,0)

# raises error 'table not defined'
```

then your working variables are encapsulated in the function and go out of scope when the function returns.

By writing a monolithic program you're essentially making all your variables global, which is generally not a good idea because it can lead to the just the problem you're describing, which is having unexpected values when they're used later on.

Anyway, short story is, next step is to pull together some acceptance tests. Kind of at the same time we should noodle out an implementation strategy, like what comes first, how big are the chunks, what comes after, etc.

The acceptance tests are in features/. They're written in Cucumber and executed by behave. Let me know if you've worked with those before. The steps (code that actually runs during the test) are in features/steps/. Maybe you can have a look around there a give me an idea how much sense it makes to you and what you need to help explain.

I'm thinking the first thing we should set our sights on is getting through the use case of adding plain text to a single header (not footer) for a single section document. That will give us more or less end-to-end capability, and everything else should be able to be built on that.

In general, the first step is test to be able to read the item, only after that to write the item. The reason being you need the read ability to test the write ability. Also write tends to build on the foundations of read so it all works out nicely. So I'm thinking the first few acceptance tests might be:

```cucumber
Given a section
  Then section.header is a Header object

Given a header having a definition
  Then header.is_linked_to_previous is False

Given a header having a definition
  Then header.text is the text contained in the header
```

And we'd proceed on from there is some rational way. These are just sketches, I think it will take a little more noodle time to get them right. In general we do one acceptance test at a time, but sometimes it makes sense to do two or three together. Getting it to the point where it runs properly but fails (due to lack of implementation), that's a commit and then it's on to unit-test/code cycles (one commit per test) until the acceptance test passes. Usually it's maybe three unit tests per acceptance test, but it varies, could be one, could be ten (but probably never twenty, that would be a test smell).

Anyway, have a look around and let me know how it goes and we'll go from there :)

There's a whole new "field decoration" framework in place now. In addition to `sklearn2pmml.decoration.CategoricalDomain` and `sklearn2pmml.decoration.ContinuousDomain` transformation types, it also applies special treatment to the `sklearn.preprocessing.Imputer` transformation type.

If you want to take advantage of this framework, then simply insert those decorators in the beginning of your transformers list:
```python
iris_mapper = sklearn_pandas.DataFrameMapper([
    (["Sepal.Length", "Sepal.Width", "Petal.Length", "Petal.Width"], [ContinuousDomain(), Imputer(), PCA(n_components = 3)]),
    ("Species", None)
])
```

@jamalex any recollection why this test dependency is pinned?

> Allows plugins to have their own package.json, and webpack.config.js - to allow for all plugin specific configuration and dependencies to be bundled into the plugin only.

Need a bit more background -- are we still going with a "core" part of Kolibri, for which plugins do not build and bundle but only depend?

@indirectlylit @jonboiser  ideally the success alert should be responsive to the screen. The main reason we're using keenUI alerts over snackbars in this case, is that because saving your facility settings can be seen as committing to one big operation (checking a bunch of stuff) and then hitting save. The user needs a visible and obvious indicator of success in this important case.

For the usage of snackbars, an example would be the actions checking different exercises to make an exam. We wouldn't incorporate success alerts for these smaller actions because the user would have to close the green alert every time something is added- which would be super obtrusive and counter-productive. Instead, we use small snackbars for quick feedback.

We also need to be careful on narrow Python builds and make sure we don't encode surrogate pairs, as `\uD800\uDC00` needs to end up unchanged.

I've been giving this one a lot of thought over the past couple of days.

I don't think that an auto-resume will work. There doesn't seem to be a facility in the websocket api for resuming a watcher, and there are a lot of ways to break and miss messages if we try to hack something together on our end.

I also don't think that an elaborate Exception surfacing scheme is necessarily called for. At least, I haven't come up with one that isn't a headache for developers who aren't me to understand.

I think that the best solution would be to attach a "monitor" class to each connection object. The monitor class would have a "status" property that would be in one of three states:

```
    1. connected
    2. errored
     3. closed
```

"connected" means that everything is healthy. "closed" means that the websocket connection is closed due to the connection.close() method being called. "errored" means that the receiver method raised an Exception for whatever reason, and our connection is no longer functional (and probably disconnected due to an unexpected network issue).

The monitor could be used in the following ways:

1) The "health" check in matrix can see that `connection.monitor.status == 'errored'`, and raise an InfraFailure.
2) A tool with an interactive user interface could have a little "connection" icon that goes from green to red when the connection fails, prompting the user to take action to reconnect.
3) A tool could track all its watchers, possibly via helpers in python-libjuju, and be able to restart them if the connection.monitor.status goes bad. The tool would be responsible for ensuring that any actions it takes on deltas in a watcher are idempotent. An auto-reconnect helper in the matrix tasks could be an eventual model for this pattern.

Right, but in Twisted you can just do "yield" inside @inlineCallbacks and it's kind-of-the-same-as re-calling your routine with a "reactor.callLater" -- it lets the reactor "do an iteration" (so, e.g., service any pending I/O or timers).

"Tail recursion" is like you're writing your own loop without a "for" or "while". So you'd chunk out your input and call something like:

```
def send_one(chunks):
   if not len(chunks):
       return
   send(chunk[0])
   reactor.callLater(0, send_one, chunks[1:])
```
So then each "reactor loop" you'll send one chunk and give other things a chance to do some work as well.  For example, imagine running 10 of these at once.

I think I hit upon a simple solution to this.

At constructor time, it is the reader's job to create the attributes. Any attributes that vary frame-by-frame can be set up as `SliceableIterable` objects themselves. Then:

```
class SliceableIterable(object):
    ...
    def __getattr__(self, key):
        # Remember this only gets called if __getattribute__ raises an
        # AttributeError. Try the ancestor object.
        attr = getattr(self._ancestor, key)
        if isinstance(attr, SliceableIterable):
            return SliceableIterable(attr, self.indices, len(self))
        else:
            return attr
```

As a demo, here's a test using letters in place of Frames.

```
def test_getattr():
    class MyList(list):
        my_attr = 'hello'
        s = SliceableIterable(list('ABCDEFGHIJ'), range(10))

    a = SliceableIterable(MyList('abcdefghij'), range(10))
    assert_letters_equal(a, list('abcdefghij'))
    assert_true(hasattr(a, 'my_attr'))
    assert_true(hasattr(a, 's'))
    assert_equal(a.my_attr, 'hello')
    with assert_raises(AttributeError):
        a[:5].nonexistent_attr

    s1 = a[::2].s
    assert_equal(list(s1), list('ACEGI'))
    s2 = a[::2][1:].s
    assert_equal(list(s2), list('CEGI'))
    s3 = a[::2][1:].s[0]
    assert_equal(s3, 'C')
```

Cool my implementation to check the response is pretty much identical to yours. As for implementing the whitelist I decided instead of listing out all the individual methods that some whole "classes" of methods (`admin`, `miner`, `shh`, `net`, `txpool`, `testing`, `evm`) could be approved without worry, let me know if you a) think that's a bad assumption, or b) don't like the idea because it doesn't require all allowable functions to be explicitly declared. 

As for the `eth` and `personal` method "classes" I left off those pertaining to sending transactions (`eth_sendTransaction`, `eth_sendRawTransaction`, `personal_signAndSendTransaction`, `personal_sendTransaction`) and individually listed all other methods. 

In order to see if the method is contained in the whitelist I created a function that returns `True` or `False` and checks to see if the function is part of one of the classes that have full approval and then if not, check if it is one of the approved fxns within either `personal` or `eth` (see code below for clarity)
```
 def check_method(method):
        root = method.split('_')[0]
        if root in whitelist:
            return True
        elif method in whitelist:
            return True
        else:
            return False
```

> `place` could be possibly used

`place` has the issue of being both a noun and a verb. That is something I've been avoiding when possible.

> `inject` is widely used in context of dependency injection, and could confuse/mislead

Since we don't use dependency injection by name anywhere, I don't expect `inject` to cause much confusion in this context. (The backend pattern is similar, but has no naming conflict).

> But I would like this "early/late" and "place" thing.

@asmeurer there was a conscious decision not to use `--prefix` in the context of environments and only support named environments.  The reason is that we can't list things like random prefixes.  Using the name only ensures that we know where the environment we're creating is.  We talked about this last fall in one of the meetings where `--prefix` becomes something that's meant as an internal plumbing tool and not something someone should actively used.

FWIW, I've considered deprecating `-n` and `--name` in favor of using regular arguments.  This comes straight from the user-experience of typing `conda env remove -y -n some-env` and how weird it looks to mix letters commonly associated with yes and no in the same command.

I think I found the cause of the problem. I had a file `$HOME/.pydistutils.cfg` which was the following:

```cfg
[install]
prefix=
```

This file sets defaults when installing with pip. It now makes sense that I had the line `error: could not create '/lib/python2.7': Permission denied` in my error log, which with variables should be `<prefix>/lib/python2.7`. Also command line options have priority which is how your work-around "solves" the problem.

Once I removed this file, I could install with `pip` again. @dckc do you have a similar file? It might be a `setup.cfg` in the current directory

Only one comment reading through the code so far...  I'd rather not put all of those executables in `bin`.  While the main `activate` and `deactivate` entry points stay in `bin`, I think the correct place for the helper/shell-specific scripts is `libexec`.  The [wiki FHS page](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Filesystem_Hierarchy_Standard) doesn't mention `libexec`, so I guess it may be somewhat non-standard. That's right. Shell hashing seems mostly unrelated here, but you are right that conda environments are designed to be isolated from one another. The real way to implement some kind of "stacking" in conda would be to reinstall each "lower" environment's packages, and provide some kind of interface on top of that to autoupdate envs. For instance, something like

```
env a: python, numpy
env b (stacked on top of env a): python, numpy, scipy

conda install -n a matplotlib  # installs matplotlib in both env a and env b
```

In other words, conda requires that env a's packages are always a subset of env b's packages when manipulating env a (and records some kind of "diff" when manipulating env b). I personally don't know if I would use the feature, but others seem to want it.

There's a lot more to how packages interact with one another than the PATH (even if conda packages did use `/usr/bin/env` in their shebang). There's also the Python path, dynamic library linking, <other language> paths, etc. You could make 90% of the cases work by setting 100 environment variables, but then you'd get a system that's 100x overengineered and still doesn't work for the remaining 10%.  

I'd reiterate: installing the same package into multiple environments with conda is extremely cheap (with the exception of cross filesystem installations and some special Linux filesystems that don't support hard linking).

In his comment above, @kalefranz described the problem well, but I don't think removing `my-virtual-env/lib` and replacing it with a symlink to `conda/lib` is a great solution.  Any packages you install to your "virtual" env will actually be installed into your "real" python interpreter (the one from conda).

Here's an alternative option:  It turns out you can *pre-emptively* create the virtual environment (as an empty directory), and then symlink the missing file *before* running `virtualenv` (`libpython2.7.dylib`).  Then `virtualenv` works as expected.

In other words:

```bash
$ mkdir -p ve/lib
$ cd ve/lib && ln -s /path/to/miniconda/lib/libpython2.7.dylib && cd -
$ virtualenv ve
```

Here's a quick-and-dirty script that does this.  Use it as if it were the `virtualenv` command.  (Caveat: This simple script misses a few corner cases, like `--help`, but you get the idea.  I haven't tested this in depth, but it seems to work.)

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# conda-virtualenv.sh
# Usage: Just like virtualenv

# Locate libpython (e.g. libpython2.7.so)
ORIG_PREFIX=$(python -c "import sys; sys.stdout.write(sys.exec_prefix)")
PYVER=$(python -c "import sys; sys.stdout.write('.'.join(map(str, sys.version_info[:2])))")
LIBPYTHON=$(ls ${ORIG_PREFIX}/lib/libpython${PYVER}*)

# Get the last arg, which is the name
# of the new virtual env directory.
for VENV_DIR; do true; done

# Pre-emptively create the env directory,
# and symlink lib/libpython2.7.so into it.
mkdir -p $VENV_DIR/lib
cd $VENV_DIR/lib && ln -s ${LIBPYTHON} && cd -

# Now call virtualenv with the user's args
virtualenv "$@"
```

Hi,

> I have little knowledge on HMM detailed work, but I guess those difference results was caused by local optimas. Please correct me if I am wrong.

EM is indeed susceptible to local optima, but I think in your case the behaviour of the algorithm is better explained by the lack of data. Starting from initial random probabilities EM simply doesn't have enough data to move away from it. 

> My question is can somebody suggest me how should I proceed to find the best optima?

Unfortunately, I'm not aware of a good solution for the case of unsupervised HMMs. A more practical version of the usual "run it N times" trick is to limit the number of iterations for each of the randomized runs. This allows you to try a lot of starting points and then pick current best one and proceed until convergence. 

For instance you could do the three runs above with `n_iter=100` and then fit the best scoring model with `n_iter=1000`. 

IIRC this modificatioin is called small EM but I don't have a reference atm.

@erasche  That is exactly what I am trying to get away from needing with work like e8d2e227583cb548d9c7c70c89163051066849fa and #26. Ideally you will just have a flat list of tool files in the source folder and some flag in ``.shed.yml`` to de-multiplex them into separate repositories during upload, lint, etc....

The point that it is tough on deployers is also well taken - but @martenson is redoing search and hopefully that will give a lot more weight to suites and this problem will also be minimized.

Back to @martenson original comment - he said "reproducibility" but I am not sure that is really the problem here. The problem with the big multi-tool repositories is that they are bad for users (given the current implementation) - if any of the tools needs a fix then all of the tools belong to a new install-able revision. This makes in inconvenient to use any of the tools with features such as workflows or re-runs if just one of them is updated.

All that said - it probably is a "best practice" to use a separate repository per tool but no one is doing it and it is not reflected in the IUC recommendations so I wouldn't feel any pressure to do things this way. Once search and planemo make it really easy - hopefully the benefits of doing it that way will become clear and then it will become a true community accepted best practice. 

tl;dr - We are either inconvenience the deployers and developers or the users. The hope is to do the former but to use planemo and tool shed search revamp to minimize the inconvenience.

Totally right about documentation. For expand_address the option is actually language code rather than country code (for Germany I suppose it doesn't matter since the two are identical). If the language is known a priori it's a good idea to pass it to expand_address. That should yield better/fewer results, as otherwise a language classifier has to be used to predict the language. For statistically close languages, if the classifier said e.g. "there's a 90% probability that it's German and a 10% probability it's Dutch" then it's possible that both German and Dutch expansions would be used.

For posterity and clarification, since I'm not sure how much the idea of "goal state" has been discussed, it's a proposed feature for Juju which provides the charm with a way to request information about what state the operator has asked Juju to get the model to (at least from the charm's perspective, i.e., what the charm would be able to see once things have settled out) regardless of the actual progress toward that state.  It's meant to be purely informative for the charm to report better status messages and possibly optimize its workload somewhat by not performing initialization or doing work that it will soon have to undo anyway (e.g., starting up a local DB to handle no DB relation, when the relation has been requested but not yet established).

Anyway, I don't know that it's any more than just an idea at this point, but it's pretty much on point for the status case I mentioned, which is why I mentioned it.

One quibble here. 

Unfortunately, validating DH parameters that are not previously specified is too expensive for clients to do. However, current attacks only work on 1024-bit RSA keys. Any similar breaks on 2048-bit RSA keys would, basically, apocalyptic across most crypto systems. (At least in a pre-quantum crypto world.)

So, the current solution for weak DH parameters is to use a cipher suite that requires 2048-bit DH.

That is very informative, I did not know that `LANG` variables may have cascading consequences.

[This helpful SE answer](http://apple.stackexchange.com/a/23175/58743) claims that the "Terminal" app configuration decides whether and which system locale environment variables are set on startup.

My best guess to what happened to us, is that after a system "restore" (dehybernation of sorts), the terminal app has not set the locale vars.
This should be confirmed by [running `locale` command](https://developer.apple.com/legacy/library/documentation/Darwin/Reference/ManPages/man1/locale.1.html) when the issue reoccurs to someone.

If the variables are not set to `**.UTF-8` then we should probably advise in the docs to set the `LANG/LC_` variables in some way. And so far the only solutions I see suggested is using the `bash_profile` ... perhaps a OSX superuser can suggest a better way?

Coincidentally, the "Troubleshooting on OS X" section suggests to "Consult your Linux distro’s documentation for information about setting these variables correctly."; however, OSX is **not** a Linux distro - docs need to be fixed here as well.

I am pretty sure this behavior is due to Z3's combined solver. We have experienced similar performance degradation compared to non-incremental solving.
Z3's combined solver (which is the default, I guess) internally combined a non-incremental solver which has a lot of preprocessings enabled and another solver instance which supports incrementality by the price of sacrificing many useful preprocessings.

As long as you do not use push/pop or issue multiple check-sat commands, the combined solver will use the non-incremental solver. Once the solver stack is used or you continue to assert formulas after having checked for satisfiability before, the combined solver resorts to the incremental solver.

What you can do is setting a timeout for the second (i.e. the incremental) solver. The corresponding solver parameter is called "solver2_timeout". Once the timeout expires in incremental queries, the combined solver aborts the incremental one and tries again with the non-incremental solver and an equivalent non-incremental query (where all formulas that have been asserted up to that point are asserted at once). All that is internal to Z3.
We are using that strategy and it performs rather well.

You can see more details and the code triggering the switching between solvers [here](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/2b0106c199a40500bb661c1afdf7818139ccd1d0/src/solver/combined_solver.cpp#L28-L42)

> I changed the name to `binary_jaccard_index`. I think this name is meaningful as the multi-class version is a bit more complicated so we can address it in another function

Sounds good to me. Even if the implementations turn out to be similar, it's easier to separate it into two functions -- especially if we want to retain the ability of providing either one-hot or integer ground truth.

> As far as I know, it is not used for training, while differentiable. See this which introduces a loss function for training which is near `jaccard_index` but not exactly the same.

Interesting. Well, the original definition wouldn't be differentiable, only the min(A,B).sum()/max(A,B).sum() formulation is.

> You do not provide per-item loss in `binary_accuracy` as well. How come it may be needed?

Right, this depends on whether we want to advertise this as an evaluation measure or as a training objective.
For an evaluation measure, the binary jaccard index *with binarized predictions* is exactly the same as the binary accuracy, so there wouldn't be a benefit of including it. With real-valued predictions it's different, but I don't know how useful/interpretable that actually is. Anyway, in this case we should compute the average, as you said, to match the interface of `binary_accuracy`.
For a training objective, it should return the item-wise loss just like `binary_crossentropy`. But in that case we'd want it to behave like a loss function, so we'd want to implement the [Jaccard distance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jaccard_index) instead (and call it `binary_jaccard_distance`).

Ah, just by chance I found that the formulation I suggested is actually given in the Wikipedia article as [Generalized Jaccard similarity and distance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jaccard_index#Generalized_Jaccard_similarity_and_distance).

https://arxiv.org/abs/1701.03056 (Section 2.2) use yet another formulation which they call Jaccard index:
`sqrt(sum((A*B)**2)) / (sum(A**2) + sum(B**2))`
They then minimize the Jaccard distance defined as 1 - Jaccard index.

https://arxiv.org/abs/1606.04797 use a similar formulation (Dice coefficient, Section 3):
`2 * sum(A*B) / (sum(A**2) + sum(B**2))`

@FabianIsensee had [another implementation](https://github.com/Lasagne/Recipes/issues/99#issuecomment-281600662) of a dice coefficient:
`2 * sum(A*B) / (sum(A) + sum(B))`

Hmm, so I'm definitely leaning towards making this a loss function for training. But I'd be happier with the min/max formulation if we found a paper using it for training. There seems no real consensus on what "the real" Jaccard distance or Dice loss should be. If we want to add one to Lasagne, we should do more literature research to collect what people have been using and what works well. Otherwise we may want to leave it up to the user to implement the variant they fancy.

> Jaccard index is the same as Intersection-over-Union (IoU) metric which is being used in semantic segmentation/Object Detection tasks. PASCAL VOC uses this metric for evaluation.

Okay, that's good, but again, it assumes discrete predictions, not real-valued ones.

@bendichter this I think at least in part a Python 2 vs. 3 issue. In Python 2 range is a list and in 3 its an iterator (i.e., what xrange is in Python2). Because of this it is not uncommon in Py3 that you have to do ``list(range(...))``.

> The W3C standard for HTML

This is comparing apples and oranges. HTML is a standard markup language not a data format. You don't need to use all parts of a language (e.g. HTML) in order to create a sensible document. However, in order to understand and interpret the content of a document (i.e., the data) you need to have the complete document and its context. In that regard:

> In fact, I would welcome making ROIs optional for RoiResponseSeries, period.

How do you know what an RoiResponseSeries means without knowing what the ROIs are?

The purpose of a data standard is not just to define what things should be called but it's also there to make sure that the data is interpretable. A data standard that makes everything optional is really not a standard because it means that folks omit a lot of information that is ultimately needed to understand the data. I understand that required fields often mean that folks have to think how to actually get that information but I don't think that is necessarily a bad thing. Folks should think about their data and not just leave out important information because it makes things easier at first. 

Ultimately, there needs to be a balance between what should be required in order to ensure that data is usable and what can be optional. In terms of what data is being required/optional NWB 2 is still very close to NWB 1.x. I don't mind if a field is optional if it is not needed to understand the data but if the information in a field is needed to understand and interpret the data than it should be required.  After all, once a field is optional you can no longer rely on the field in your data analysis. Optional fields are a double-edged-sword, if a field is optional it may make it "easier" to write data but it also makes it harder to write consistent APIs, analysis codes, and tools that may rely on or change behavior based on the corresponding information.

Sending a message:

- Send the message `CMsgClientFriendMsg`
- `steamid` is the steamid64 you want to send the message to
- `chat_entry_type` is the same as before -- `2` means the sender has started typing in the chat window, `1` means a message has been sent
- `message` is the message that you want to send
- `rtime32_server_timestamp` - The time the client sent the message (in [Unix time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix_time))

Putting this all together you could write a simple "copycat":

    @client.on(EMsg.ClientFriendMsgIncoming)
    def onMessage(msg):
        messageText = msg.body.message.rstrip().strip("\0")

        if messageText:
            sendMsg = MsgProto(EMsg.ClientFriendMsg)
            sendMsg.body.steamid = msg.body.steamid_from
            sendMsg.body.chat_entry_type = 1
            sendMsg.body.message = messageText
            sendMsg.body.rtime32_server_timestamp = int(time.time())

            client.send(sendMsg)

Unfortunately the DiracAPI doesn't quite do what we want. You can give ```getReplicas``` a list of lfns. You can also give ```getAccessURL``` a list of LFNs but you have to also give it a single valid SE.

So what we can do is make a list of the unique SEs that the files in the list are at:
```python
#get all the replicas:
reps = execute('getReplicas(%s)' %lfns)
#Get the SEs
SEs = []
for lf in reps['Value']['Successful']:
    thisSE = reps['Value']['Successful'][lf].values()
    if thisSE in SEs:
        continue
    else:
        SEs.append(thisSE)
```
The we iterate over this list of SEs with the getAccessURL and avoid doing it twice for the same LFN:
```python
processedLFNs = []
myURLs = []
for SE in SEs:
         thisSEFiles = execute('getAccessURL(%s, "%s")' % (lfns , 'CERN-USER'))['Value']['Successful']
            for file in thisSEFiles.keys():
                 if file in processedLFNs:
                     continue
                 else:
                   myURLs.append(thisSEFiles[file])
```

It is not quite ideal because it still has to run a Dirac command for each SE but it should be a lot quicker than previously as I assume there are far fewer SEs than files.

Your solution with dirac-dms-lfn-accessURL is probably the quickest in terms of speed but this saves having to mess around with formatting command line output.

The setup examples given in the multiple camera paper (all cams connected to a hub, or all cams connected directly to USB ports) do seem to indicate a preference for all cameras being connected to the same PC.

As the paper suggests, the ideal setup is to use multiple individual USB ports instead of a hub in order to avoid more than one device being shared on the same USB controller.  The paper correctly points out though that some PC devices take the shortcut of pairing individual ports on a shared internal hub instead of each having their own USB controller.

I would suppose that if a slave camera is connected to a different PC to the master then the PC that the master was connected to would not know of the slave camera's existence.

USB supports the chaining of up to 127 devices on the same PC, in theory.  In practice, you could not actually connect 127 cameras because you would need to connect together a pile of USB hubs, and each of those hubs claims some of the 127 addresses even before a device is attached to the hub.  So you could probably chain 50+ cameras together on the same PC, but in practice the performance would probably be so slow as to be unusable.  

The ideal in mass-camera setups would be to only have several cameras at a time active, and cycle through the cameras sequentially, turning them on and off in batches as capture snapshots are taken until the cycle gets back to the first cameras again and begins the cycle anew.  This likely would not be feasible for syncing though, where you want all the cameras active at the same time.  

It might become more feasible on modern, faster USB standards like USB 3.1, but there would still be the issue of the poorer performance on a  hub's shared USB controllers compared to connecting each to its own individual USB controller.

Performance aside, if you want to know whether or not GCS network egress is free, here's a helpful shell function:

```sh
is_gcs_free() {
  GCS_BUCKET=$1
  GCE_ZONE=$(curl -sfL metadata.google.internal/0.1/meta-data/zone) || { echo Not in GCE fleet >&2; return 1; }
  GCE_REGION=$(printf %s\\n "${GCE_ZONE}" | sed -n 's!.*/\([^-/]*-[^-/]*\).*!\1!p')
  GCP_TOKEN=$(curl -sfLH Metadata-Flavor:Google metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/default/token | sed -n 's/.*"access_token": *"\([^"]*\)".*/\1/p')
  GCS_REGION=$(curl -sfLH "Authorization:Bearer ${GCP_TOKEN}" "https://www.googleapis.com/storage/v1/b/$1" | sed -n 's/.*"location": *"\([^"]*\)".*/\1/p' | tr A-Z a-z)
  [ $(expr "${GCE_REGION}" : "${GCS_REGION}") -gt 0 ]
}
```

Thinking about this for an hour, the best solution I can come up with is a bit gross. The proposal is to add a new optional parameter to `IDisplay` methods called `new` which defaults to `True`. If a question is new, we check if both `sys.stdin.isatty()` and `sys.stdout.isatty()` are `True`. If they are, we proceed as normal and ask the question. If they're not, we fall back to a default.

I'd love to be able to just do this for all questions by just setting `config.noninteractive_mode` based on the the `isatty` checks, but I think this will change workflows for people in some corner cases.

@erikrose zope.component and Zope projects in general tend to use setuptools style namespace packages. So the `__init__` in `src/zope` contains something like `__import__('pkg_resources').declare_namespace(__name__)`.

There are also some other Zope projects using `pkg_resources` features to get version information about installed packages.

I forgot when namespace packages got added to Python Core, but they aren't in Python 2.7, and that's still supported.

Otherwise you'd be correct, declaring setuptools as a `setup_requires` dependency is useless, as that's a setuptools feature in the first place. But for the namespace package support, it's an actual runtime (install_requires) dependency.

If the src folder contains a file `__init__.py`, the src folder itself should be the base package. If there is no such file, the name of the src folder should be skipped.

Example with `__init__.py`:
- `src/`
  - `__init__.py <frozen src>`
  - `subpkg/`
    - `__init__.py <frozen src.subpkg>`
    - `submod.py <frozen src.subpkg.submod>`
  - `mod.py <frozen src.mod>`

Example without `__init__.py`:
- `src/`
  - `subpkg/`
    - `__init__.py <frozen subpkg>`
    - `submod.py <frozen subpkg.submod>`
  - `mod.py <frozen mod>`

I suppose we can make `no-wait` a queue option. That is to say, we can make `wait` an default option so the local task queue will by default wait. Then for remote `PBS_TaskEngine` we can set `wait: False` so that we do not wait for tasks for that particular queue.

In this way we get around of the default `-w` problem and saves an option.

When `-q` is not specified, `task` will still be generated and be executed locally, and in this case `remote(file)` is simply `file`. However, using `remote` target in a step without `task` is currently undefined, meaning it most likely will not work.

Global `remote` sounds like an interesting idea....

Which version of streamlink are you using? You need at least `0.6.0` to be able to watch UStream streams. Most UStream channels work, there are a few that do not (ones that don't allow mobile streaming, which is what streamlink uses). I tested a few from the [top live streams](http://www.ustream.tv/explore/all), and they all worked. 

The [ISS HDEV channel](http://www.ustream.tv/channel/iss-hdev-payload) says "OFF AIR" on the website, and it is playing a [video](http://www.ustream.tv/recorded/104984964) in place of the live stream. The ustreamtv plugin only supports loading the live stream from a channel page - off air content is ignored, if you want to watch archived videos you need to pass the URL of the video to streamlink, eg. http://www.ustream.tv/recorded/104984964. The reason off air content is not played is because it would not be possible for streamlink to switch back to the live stream when it came back online.

I did some investigations about this issue and found out why I had such results. According to PDF specifications:

> **User Space**
>To avoid the device-dependent effects of specifying objects in device space, PDF
defines a device-independent coordinate system that always bears the same relationship
to the current page, regardless of the output device on which printing or
displaying occurs. This device-independent coordinate system is called user
space. 
>The user space coordinate system is initialized to a default state for each page of a
document. The CropBox entry in the page dictionary specifies the rectangle of
user space corresponding to the visible area of the intended output medium (display
window or printed page). The positive x axis extends horizontally to the
right and the positive y axis vertically upward, as in standard mathematical practice
(subject to alteration by the Rotate entry in the page dictionary). The length
of a unit along both the x and y axes is set by the UserUnit entry (PDF 1.6) in the
page dictionary (see Table 3.27). If that entry is not present or supported, the default
value of 1⁄72 inch is used. This coordinate system is called **default user space**.

Ah-hah, I thought of a case where batched accept makes a difference:

Say we have a mixed workload, involving a CPU-bound "background task" that regularly `sleep(0)`s to let other stuff run, plus a server task that `accept`s new connections. Suppose the CPU bound task `sleep(0)`s every 20 ms, and that each incoming connection can be completely serviced in 0.1 ms. Then in principle our server can handle 1000 connections per second (albeit at that point the background task doesn't get to run at all), but if we yield after every `accept` then we end up only servicing 1000 / 20 = 50 connections / second.

I think this situation is kind of problematic all around... it would be difficult to service a connection in 0.1 ms in Python, and only yielding every 20 ms is not so great, and if the goal is that the heavy background task shouldn't interfere with the light connection tasks, then wouldn't the Right solution be to make a better scheduling algorithm (#32) or just assign a low static priority to the background task? But I'm sure this kind of thing happens, at least at times.

I guess that from this point of view, the point of a batched accept is that normally an accept loop is a *very* light task (it does very little work between calls to accept), so standard round-robin scheduling will tend to implicitly assign it a very low priority (each of its individual time slices end up being very small compared to other tasks, and round-robin says that they all get the same *number* of time slices). So it's a heuristic way of compensating for a particular failure mode of the not-very-clever round-robin scheduler.

## 导出 PDF 功能

一直以来 PDF 输出的问题困恼着 pyecharts 用户。目前的解决方案是结合 pyecharts-snapshot 来生成静态图片，然后输出。用户需要做两件事情：

第一就是装 [pyecharts-snapshot 0.1.4+ 和 phanomjs-prebuilt](https://github.com/pyecharts/pyecharts-snapshot#installation)

第二就是在 notebook 的最开始添上一下语句。这样就可以输出图片称 PDF 了。

```python
from pyecharts import configure

configure(output_image='png')
```

## 导出的 HTML 文件， 在没有互联网，怎么样打开图片

这个时候， 你也需要装 pyecharts-snapshot 0.1.4+ 。你需要用 `renderer` 参数让 pyecharts 0.4.2+ 生成 svg 图片并且加上下面的语句。

```python
from pyecharts import configure

configure(output_image='svg')
```

同时，请注意，这个配置也是可以输出 PDF 的。可就是输出的图片不仅需要 Inkscape，输出图片效果很不好。所以不推荐用 `output_image='svg'`。
